metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a proving command for AC4 that demonstrates the drift check fires — e.g., temporarily comment out one intercept arm check in the generator and assert it exits non-zero, similar to the AC3 CAUGHT sequence: `python scripts/gen_cli_reference.py --check-intercepts 2>&1 | grep -q 'drift' && echo DRIFT_CAUGHT || echo DRIFT_MISS`.
findings.finding: AC4 ('the intercept list in the generator matches the _cli/__init__.py intercept ladder') has no proving command that actually exercises or verifies the drift check fires when a new intercept arm is added. The AC only states the check 'is' present, making it non-measurably stated and unverifiable without reading the source.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix the AC2 grep to anchor on a markdown heading or quoted command name (e.g., `grep -qF "### $c"` or `grep -qE "^#+[[:space:]]$c([[:space:]]|$)"`) to eliminate substring false-positives for short intercept command names like 'review' and 'config'.
findings.finding: The completeness-proof commands in AC2 use `grep -qF "$c"` (fixed-string, substring match), which produces a false-positive for short command names that appear as substrings of longer ones. For example, `grep -qF 'review' docs/cli-reference.md` will succeed if only 'review-code' or 'review-plan' is present, silently masking the absence of the standalone 'review' command. Similarly, 'config' or 'prompt' could match incidentally in prose or other command output text.
findings.suggested_fix: Change both `grep -qF "$c"` calls to `grep -qE "(^|[^a-z-])${c}([^a-z-]|$)"` or, better, rely on a structural anchor matching the generated markdown heading pattern, e.g., `grep -qF "## ${c}"` (or whatever heading prefix the generator uses), so that 'review' only matches the '## review' section heading.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC3) that verifies `.github/workflows/test.yml` contains the drift-gate step, e.g. by grepping the workflow file for the generator invocation or by confirming the CI job name exists.
findings.finding: The `.github/workflows/test.yml` modification is listed as a named deliverable in both Scope and Files, but no AC verifies that the CI gate was actually wired in the workflow file. AC3 only validates the `--check` flag locally; an implementer could satisfy all five ACs without touching `.github/workflows/test.yml`.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (all 5 items); Scope section ('Wire a CI drift gate in `.github/workflows/test.yml`'); Files: `.github/workflows/test.yml`
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC such as: '[ ] `.github/workflows/test.yml` contains a step that runs `python scripts/gen_cli_reference.py --check` (or equivalent `git diff --exit-code` gate): `grep -q gen_cli_reference .github/workflows/test.yml && echo OK`'
Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the generator must raise/exit-non-zero if any `rebar <cmd> --help` subprocess exits with a non-zero code OR produces empty stdout, so failures are never silently swallowed into the output file.
findings.finding: The 16 subprocess calls to `rebar <cmd> --help` have no specified error-handling: if a subprocess exits non-zero or produces empty/garbled output, the generator may silently emit a blank or incomplete entry for that intercept command, producing a quietly broken reference doc without failing the build.
findings.location: Scope section: 'family 2 via the explicit intercept list + `rebar <cmd> --help`'
findings.suggested_fix: In `gen_cli_reference.py`, use `subprocess.run([...], check=True, capture_output=True)` and assert `result.stdout.strip()` is non-empty before writing each intercept entry; exit non-zero with a descriptive message if either condition fails.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the generator's error-handling policy for subprocess failures when capturing `rebar <cmd> --help` (e.g., abort with a clear error message and non-zero exit if any call fails or returns empty output).
findings.finding: The plan specifies running `rebar <cmd> --help` via subprocess for all 16 intercept-arm commands but does not specify how the generator handles subprocess failures (non-zero exit, command not on PATH, timeout, or empty output) — a non-happy-path that could silently produce a partial or corrupt reference document.
findings.location: Scope section — 'family 2 via the explicit intercept list + `rebar <cmd> --help`'; scripts/gen_cli_reference.py (new)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope section: 'If any `rebar <cmd> --help` subprocess returns a non-zero exit code or empty stdout, the generator MUST abort immediately with a descriptive error message and non-zero exit, so CI surfaces the failure rather than silently committing a partial document.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `gerrit-verify.yaml` to the Files section and Scope, specifying that the CLI-reference drift gate is mirrored there (matching the env-registry pattern the plan cites, and satisfying `test_no_drift_script_gate_is_verified_only_in_branch_ci`).
findings.finding: The plan says it mirrors the env-registry gate but its Files section lists only `.github/workflows/test.yml`, omitting `gerrit-verify.yaml`. The existing test `test_no_drift_script_gate_is_verified_only_in_branch_ci` (tests/unit/test_ci_workflow_parity.py:72–86) auto-detects any `scripts/*.py` invoked in test.yml that is absent from gerrit-verify.yaml and fails the build — so the plan as written will break that test on merge.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `.github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml` to the Files list and add a sentence to the Scope CI bullet: 'Also mirror in `.github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml` (same pattern as the env-registry gate, required by `test_no_drift_script_gate_is_verified_only_in_branch_ci`).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete mechanism for the AC-4 intercept-list drift check (e.g., 'grep for `argv\[0\] ==` string literals in `_cli/__init__.py` and compare the extracted set to the generator's hardcoded list, failing if they differ'), so the implementation is unambiguous and reviewable.
findings.finding: The mechanism for the intercept-list drift check (AC 4) is unspecified — the plan asserts 'a drift check in the generator or gate asserts this' but does not specify HOW the generator will verify that its hardcoded 16-command list still matches the `if argv[0] == '...'` intercept ladder in `_cli/__init__.py`. Parsing Python source for this pattern is non-trivial and could be fragile (regex, AST, or text search each have failure modes), yet the plan leaves the implementation entirely open.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC 4; Scope section ('a drift check in the generator or gate asserts this')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note to the Scope section: 'The drift check extracts intercept command names by scanning `_cli/__init__.py` for the pattern `argv[0] == "<cmd>"` (via `re.findall`) and asserts the resulting set equals the generator's hardcoded `INTERCEPT_COMMANDS` frozenset, raising an error with a diff if not.' This makes the mechanism concrete and testable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete mechanism for the intercept-drift check: e.g., 'the generator parses `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py` with a regex (`argv\[0\] == "(\w[\w-]*)"`) to extract all intercept names at runtime, then asserts its hardcoded INTERCEPT_CMDS list matches the extracted set, raising an error if they differ.'
findings.finding: The plan mandates an intercept-drift check ('a drift check in the generator or gate asserts the list still matches the _cli/__init__.py intercept ladder') but never specifies the concrete mechanism — e.g., whether the generator should regex-parse __init__.py for `argv[0] == "..."` patterns, import a to-be-added extraction helper, or use another approach. Without this, an implementer may write a check that never actually reads __init__.py and silently passes even when the ladder diverges.
findings.location: Scope section / AC line: 'The intercept list in the generator matches the _cli/__init__.py intercept ladder (a drift check in the generator or gate asserts this...)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope section: 'The generator extracts the live intercept set from `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py` by scanning for `argv[0] == "<name>"` patterns (regex), compares them to its hardcoded INTERCEPT_CMDS constant, and aborts with a descriptive error if they differ — this is the drift guard.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state which job in test.yml receives the CLI-reference drift-gate step (e.g., 'add to the `test` matrix job after the Env-var registry drift gate step, mirroring it exactly') to remove placement ambiguity.
findings.finding: The plan says to wire the CI drift gate in `.github/workflows/test.yml` 'mirroring the env-registry gate' but does not specify which job the new step should be added to. The env-registry gate lives in the `test` job (the matrix job), meaning it runs on every OS/Python version combination — potentially an unintentional cost for a documentation drift check.
findings.location: Scope section: 'Wire a CI drift gate in .github/workflows/test.yml mirroring the env-registry gate'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief positive rationale for why Family 2 help is captured via subprocess (`rebar <cmd> --help`) rather than in-process argparse invocation — e.g. 'subprocess is used so the captured output is byte-for-byte identical to what a user sees, including argparse formatting and prog= prefix, without coupling the generator to each command's internal parser import path.'
findings.finding: The plan does not state a positive rationale for why Family 2 (intercept-arm commands) help is captured via `rebar <cmd> --help` subprocess rather than calling each command's argparse parser directly in-process (which would avoid a live `rebar` entrypoint dependency during generation and make the generator more testable in isolation).
findings.location: Scope section — 'family 2 via the explicit intercept list + `rebar <cmd> --help`'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, add one sentence after 'family 2 via the explicit intercept list + `rebar <cmd> --help`': 'Subprocess capture is used so the generator produces the exact user-visible output (argparse-formatted, including `prog=` prefix) without importing each command's internal parser — keeping the generator decoupled from the intercept implementations and consistent with how the reference will be consumed.'

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'single source of truth in `src/rebar/_commands`' for deriving command names, usage strings, and flags, but the actual source of truth is `src/rebar/_cli/help/*.txt` (static package-data text files) plus dispatch frozensets in `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py`. No structured CLI registry exists in `_commands`.
findings.location: What section: 'derive the command list, usage strings, and flags from the CLI registry / `--help` (single source of truth in `src/rebar/_commands`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Update the plan to say: 'derive the command inventory from the dispatch frozensets in `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py` and the static help text in `src/rebar/_cli/help/*.txt`; for commands with native argparse parsers (review, llm, workflow, etc.), invoke each with `--help` and capture stdout.' Remove the reference to a CLI registry in `_commands`.

findings.finding: The plan specifies 'single source of truth in src/rebar/_commands' as the data source for the generator, but the actual authoritative command enumeration lives across three locations: (1) src/rebar/_cli/help/*.txt (known_subcommands() — the canononical list used by the CLI itself), (2) src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py (intercept arms for reconcile, review, review-code, scan-spec, verify-completion, review-plan, sign-review, enrich, explain, verify-commit-ticket, workflow, llm, jira-onboard, prompt, criteria, config, plus frozenset dispatch groups), and (3) src/rebar/_commands/__init__.py (_REGISTRY + _ARGV_REGISTRY, leaf-write only). A generator targeting only src/rebar/_commands would miss the majority of commands.
findings.location: What section: 'derive the command list, usage strings, and flags from the CLI registry / --help (single source of truth in src/rebar/_commands)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'single source of truth in src/rebar/_commands' with: 'derive the command list from src/rebar/_cli/help/*.txt (the authoritative enumeration) and usage strings from the same package-data files; harvest arity/flag metadata from src/rebar/_commands.__init__._REGISTRY and _ARGV_REGISTRY for the leaf-write subset.'

findings.finding: The plan names 'src/rebar/_commands' as the single source of truth for the command list, but this covers only ~13 of ~50 CLI commands. The actual authoritative source is src/rebar/_cli/help/*.txt (consumed by known_subcommands()); an additional ~15+ commands live in src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py intercept arms (reconcile, review, review-plan, workflow, llm, jira-onboard, config, explain, etc.) and read-path frozensets — none of these are in src/rebar/_commands. A generator built against _commands alone would produce an incomplete reference, and AC3 ('Adding/removing a CLI command or flag changes the generated output') would silently fail for any command in the _cli intercept layer.
findings.location: What section: 'derive the command list, usage strings, and flags from the CLI registry / --help (single source of truth in src/rebar/_commands)'
findings.suggested_fix: Point gen_cli_reference.py at the package data in src/rebar/_cli/help/: enumerate known_subcommands() for the command list, read each <cmd>.txt for the usage string, and read overview.txt for the top-level structure. This gives complete, already-maintained coverage with zero subprocess invocation needed.

findings.finding: The plan asserts a single source of truth in 'src/rebar/_commands' but the actual CLI command surface is split across at least five distinct structures: _REGISTRY, _ARGV_REGISTRY (both in _commands/__init__.py), seven+ frozensets in _cli/__init__.py routing reads/gates/lifecycle/bridge/IO commands, and per-command argparse parsers. The generator design needed to cover the full surface is materially more complex than the env-registry prior art (which had one clean scan target), and the plan does not acknowledge this fragmentation.
findings.location: What section: 'derive the command list, usage strings, and flags from the CLI registry / --help (single source of truth in src/rebar/_commands)'

findings.finding: The plan names `src/rebar/_commands` as the 'single source of truth' for the command list, but that module only registers ~13 leaf-write commands (`_REGISTRY` + `_ARGV_REGISTRY`). The actual full CLI surface (~50 subcommands) already lives as machine-readable package-data in `src/rebar/_cli/help/*.txt`, enumerable in-process via `_help.known_subcommands()` and `_help.subcommand_help(sub)`. Specifying `_commands` as the source would cause the generator to rebuild partial command-discovery from scratch while missing the majority of the CLI, when the full, authoritative enumeration already exists and is already used by tests.
findings.location: Plan body: 'derive the command list, usage strings, and flags from the CLI registry / `--help` (single source of truth in `src/rebar/_commands`)'
findings.suggested_fix: In `scripts/gen_cli_reference.py`, use `from rebar._cli._help import known_subcommands, subcommand_help, overview` to enumerate all subcommands and their usage strings. This reuses the existing machine-readable package-data already pinned by golden tests, rather than partially rebuilding command discovery from `_commands`.

findings.finding: The plan's named source of truth — 'CLI registry / `--help` (single source of truth in `src/rebar/_commands`)' — is materially incomplete. The actual CLI surface spans at least three distinct locations: (1) `_REGISTRY` + `_ARGV_REGISTRY` in `src/rebar/_commands/__init__.py` (covers ~13 leaf-write commands); (2) hard-coded `if argv[0] == <sub>` intercept arms in `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py` (covers `review`, `review-code`, `scan-spec`, `workflow`, `llm`, `reconcile`, `jira-onboard`, `prompt`, `criteria`, `config`, `verify-commit-ticket`, `sign-review`, `explain`, `enrich`, and others — none present in either registry); and (3) static `.txt` package-data files in `src/rebar/_cli/help/` (51 files — the authoritative known-subcommand catalog via `known_subcommands()`). A generator targeting only `src/rebar/_commands` will silently omit the majority of user-visible subcommands from the reference doc.
findings.location: What / Files section: 'derive the command list, usage strings, and flags from the CLI registry / `--help` (single source of truth in `src/rebar/_commands`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Use `rebar._cli._help.known_subcommands()` (which reads `src/rebar/_cli/help/*.txt`) as the command catalog, and read each `<sub>.txt` for the usage string. This is already the complete, maintained, golden-pinned source of truth — the generator becomes a straightforward iteration over the package-data directory, exactly mirroring the env-registry scanner's proven pattern.

findings.finding: AC3 ('Adding/removing a CLI command or flag changes the generated output (verified)') has no proving command. 'Verified' is a hedge that does not specify who verifies it, by what mechanism, or what command produces evidence — an executor cannot confirm this criterion is met.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: '- [ ] Adding/removing a CLI command or flag changes the generated output (verified).'
findings.suggested_fix: AC3 proving command: `echo 'stub' >> src/rebar/_commands/__init__.py && python scripts/gen_cli_reference.py && git diff --exit-code docs/cli-reference.md; echo 'EXPECTED non-zero exit (diff detected)'; git checkout src/rebar/_commands/__init__.py docs/cli-reference.md`

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
